body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

In my code, I would like to be able to declare method parameters as having the same type as `self`, for the purpose of declaring methods that accept an instance of the same class of the current object (or a subclass). Python has this type annotation as `typing.Self`.

My real-world use case for this is for some experiments I'm doing to try to improve matrix arithmetic in SageMath. In Sage, we have several base classes for matrices, and our coercion system takes care of ensuring that by the time a method to multiply two matrices is called, the two input matrices have the same type and are defined over the same base ring. I would like to be able to declare a method in a way that reflects this. While I'm focused on matrices here, I think this applies to many parts of Sage, and would also probably be useful for other projects with complex type systems.

A simplified example of what I wish I could do (yes, technically this violates the Liskov substitution principle, but not in a way that can lead to any problems for users since the public API handles the type conversions already):

```cython
cdef class AbstractMatrix:
    cdef AbstractMatrix _mul_(self, AbstractMatrix other):
        ...

cdef class DenseMatrix(AbstractMatrix):
    cdef DenseMatrix _mul_(self, DenseMatrix other):
        ...
```

Currently Cython gives a compilation error that the definition of `_mul_` does not match the definition in the superclass. Which is fair enough. But we can accomplish something similar to the above example with Python type annotations, and I'd like to be able to do something similar in Cython:

```python
from typing import Self

class AbstractMatrix:
    def _mul_(self, other: Self) -> Self:  # other must be an instance or subclass of AbstractMatrix
        ...

def DenseMatrix(AbstractMatrix):
    def _mul_(self, other: Self) -> Self:  # other must be an instance or subclass of DenseMatrix
        ...
```

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Have some way to declare a `cdef`/`cpdef` method whose types do not match the declaration of the same method in the parent class, at least for the specific case of declaring a method that accepts instances of the current class.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

I could do this with regular `def` methods, but I prefer to have C methods for speed.

### Additional context

I've tried to find a way to do something like this in Cython and failed to find a way to do it. If there is some way that I missed however, I think it would be worth explaining somewhere in the documentation.

comment_data.body:
The easiest way to represent it currently is probably just:

```
class AbstractMatrix:
    cdef AbstractMatrix _mul_(self, AbstractMatrix other):
        ...

def DenseMatrix(AbstractMatrix):
    cdef DenseMatrix _mul_(self, AbstractMatrix other):
        if not isinstance(other, DenseMatrix):
            raise TypeError
        ...
```

(Not sure off the top of my head if we're OK with the varying return types though. But if not then just mark the return type as AbstractMatrix too)

Obviously this is more verbose than if it was native to Cython. But it's substantially what Cython would have to do for you.

title: [ENH] Add Cython equivalent of Python typing.Self
